problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem C (data/"big data" track): Sunshine Company is entering the online marketplace for three products (microwave ovens, hair dryers, baby pacifiers) and provided Amazon review data (star_rating, review text, helpful_votes/total_votes, vine, verified_purchase, dates). The team had to (a) mine star ratings, helpfulness ratings, and review text to recommend a data-driven sales strategy, (b) identify which reviews/ratings indicate product success vs failure and how they combine into a reputation measure, (c) detect whether reviews are influenced by prior ratings/reviews over time, and (d) determine whether specific text quality-descriptors are tied to rating levels. The paper decomposes this into four chained models plus a brand-recommendation scoring system and a manager letter.
models_used: Model 1 - Multinomial (ordinal-style) Logistic Regression: helpfulness rating bucketed into 4 classes, regressed in 3 nested blocks on star rating, star rating squared (SS), review length (count), length squared (CS), emotional compound score, plus vine and verified_purchase dummies. Finds U-shaped star->helpfulness and inverted-U length->helpfulness.
NLTK VADER sentiment analysis: rule/lexicon-based SentimentIntensityAnalyzer producing a 'compound' score in [-1,1] for every review, used as a feature across all four models.
Pearson correlation analysis + correlation heatmaps among star rating, compound, count, helpfulness rating as exploratory groundwork before regression.

Model 2 - LDA (Latent Dirichlet Allocation, Blei 2003) topic model: run separately on positive and negative review subsets per product to extract 3 latent topics x top-10 words; topics hand-mapped to 5 product attributes (quality, price, size, service, appearance).

Cosine text-similarity scoring engine ('traversing/ergodic search' over an undirected complex network of reviews): per attribute, pick the highest-VADER-score review as a 'SIGNIFICANT REVIEW', score every other review by cosine similarity to it; vine/verified reviews up-weighted x2. Produces per-attribute index scores.

AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process): expert-scored 6x6 judgment matrix over the 5 attributes + star rating, eigenvector weighting with CI/CR consistency test (<0.1), giving per-product attribute weights.

Hierarchical/System clustering (Q-cluster, Euclidean distance + cosine similarity, SPSS): clusters product 'parents' into 10 grades on the weighted attribute scores; class-1 brands recommended to the company.

Model 3 - Time series: reputation defined as 0.7*mean compound + 0.3*mean star rating; AR(1) autoregression fit by MLE with ACF/PACF order identification, AIC/BIC over- and under-fitting checks, additive seasonal decomposition; forecasts seasonal reputation peaks per product.

Model 4 - Distributed Lag Model via Almon method: 2nd-order Almon polynomial, lag 3, regressing current review compound on current and lagged star ratings (Z-transform to alpha_0..alpha_2) to show current reviews are influenced by past ratings/reviews; ties text descriptors to rating synchrony.
Sensitivity analysis: varied the 0.7/0.3 reputation weighting to 0.6/0.4 and 0.8/0.2, showing AR(1) coefficients shift <1.8%.
key_techniques: Heavy, problem-specific text preprocessing: non-alphabetic/emoji tokens mapped to 'five stars', PyEnchant spell correction, lowercasing, and cosine-similarity redundancy removal that drops off-topic reviews below a similarity threshold (e.g. blanket/basket reviews under a pacifier).
Feature engineering of squared terms (SS, CS) specifically to test and reveal non-monotonic U / inverted-U relationships rather than assuming linearity.
Nested/block regression (Model 1->2->3 adding intuitive, content, then reviewer-credibility features) so each variable group's marginal contribution is visible.
Constructing a derived target variable Hr = helpful_votes/total_votes and a composite reputation = 0.7 compound + 0.3 star.
Mapping unsupervised LDA topics onto a fixed 5-attribute business taxonomy (quality/price/size/service/appearance) so the model output is directly actionable.
Credibility weighting: vine + verified_purchase reviews doubled in the index-scoring engine and used as regressors, operationalizing the trust assumptions.
AHP consistency test (CI/CR<0.1) to defend otherwise-subjective expert weights.
AR-order discipline: ACF/PACF identification confirmed by AIC/BIC minimization at p=1.
Almon polynomial transform to reduce multicollinearity among lagged regressors in the distributed-lag model.
Closing the loop to the prompt: a manager Letter translating negative LDA topic words ('heavy','noise','smoking','hot','small','service') into concrete redesign recommendations.
why_it_won: "It directly and completely answers every sub-question of Problem C with a distinct, named model, then ties all four back into business deliverables (brand recommendations, seasonal sales timing, review-management advice) and a manager letter. Judges reward this kind of explicit prompt-coverage plus an end-to-end pipeline (raw data -> preprocessing -> sentiment -> topics -> weighted scoring -> clustering -> forecasting -> lag causality). The team showed genuine data-science breadth across the C-track skill set (NLP, regression, unsupervised topic modeling, MCDM weighting, clustering, time series, econometric lag models) while keeping each step interpretable and decision-relevant. Strong signals of rigor stand out: significance stars on every coefficient, AHP consistency testing, AIC/BIC order selection, a real sensitivity analysis on the reputation weights, and the counter-intuitive but defended U-shaped finding that they explicitly contrast with prior literature (Mudambi & Schuff). The actionable, attribute-level redesign advice derived straight from negative-topic words is exactly the 'so what' judges look for."
section_highlights: "Summary front-loads all four findings (U-relationships, brand scoring/clustering, seasonal reputation forecast, lag influence) so a triage judge sees the whole arc in one paragraph. A two-tier flowchart (Figure 1) and a dedicated data-preprocessing flowchart give a visual roadmap. Each model gets its own numbered section with a consistent rhythm: motivation -> equations -> table of fitted coefficients with significance -> interpretation. Heavy use of visuals (correlation heatmaps, box/stacked-bar plots, word clouds, sequence/forecast charts) keeps a data-heavy paper readable. A standalone Sensitivity Analysis section and an explicit Strengths/Weaknesses self-assessment signal maturity. The Letter to the Marketing Director is concrete and per-product, converting model output into named defects and fixes, which makes the work feel deployed rather than academic."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The helpfulness-rating dependent variable is bucketed by 'compound' thresholds in Equation 4 (0-0.25, ... 0.75-1), not by Hr — an internal inconsistency between the stated target (Hr) and the cut-points used, suggesting a mislabeled/confused dependent variable.
AHP weights come from an expert-scored judgment matrix — the team admits this is subjective; weights are essentially asserted, not learned from data, which weakens the brand-ranking objectivity.

Vine and verified_purchase were kept as regressors even though the team notes almost no vine members exist in the sample, so those coefficients carry little real information (kept for 'completeness').
Time series uses only monthly aggregates and an AR(1)/additive-seasonal model; it ignores holidays and special events and the team concedes long-horizon reputation forecasts are unreliable.
Reported R^2 ~0.90-0.95 on the AR(1) reputation series is suspiciously high for monthly review aggregates and likely reflects autocorrelation/trend rather than predictive skill.
The distributed-lag 'causality' claim is correlational: Almon regression of compound on lagged stars shows association, not that prior ratings cause current reviews.
Aggressive preprocessing choices (mapping all emoji to 'five stars', similarity-thresholded review deletion) can inject bias and discard signal, with the threshold never reported.
Cosine-similarity index scoring against a single highest-sentiment 'significant review' per attribute is ad hoc and sensitive to that one anchor review.
Star ratings are treated as a cardinal numeric variable (squared, averaged) despite being ordinal.
reusable_patterns: Four-model decomposition mapped one-to-one onto the prompt's sub-questions — a clean template for any multi-part data problem.
Standard text pipeline for review-mining problems: clean/normalize -> VADER compound score -> LDA topics -> map topics to a fixed business attribute taxonomy -> per-attribute scoring.
Composite-index recipe: derive target (Hr), build a weighted multi-attribute score, fix the weights with AHP+consistency test, then cluster entities into quality tiers for a ranked recommendation.
Add squared feature terms whenever you suspect (or want to surface) U-shaped / saturating effects, and interpret the sign pair to claim the shape.
Nested/block regression to expose each feature group's incremental effect and to justify model complexity.
Time-series discipline checklist: define composite metric, ACF/PACF order ID, confirm with AIC/BIC, decompose seasonality, then forecast and read business timing off the peaks.
Almon polynomial distributed-lag setup as a ready tool for 'does the past influence the present' sub-questions.
Always include a sensitivity analysis on any hand-set weight (here the 0.7/0.3 reputation split) and report the % change to defend robustness.
End with a stakeholder Letter that converts negative-topic keywords into named, per-product design fixes — the actionable 'so what' that wins C-track.
